Adam Neumann.blurb: Co-founder of WeWork, the co-working company that burned through $47 billion in valuation after Neumann's management chaos, self-dealing, and erratic behavior forced him out.

Bill Ackman.blurb: Hedge fund billionaire and Harvard alum who coordinated a CEO campaign to dox and blacklist student protesters, then used donor leverage to oust a university president.

Bill Gates.blurb: Microsoft co-founder and philanthropist who cultivated a multi-year relationship with Jeffrey Epstein after his sex trafficking conviction, attending private dinners and flying on his plane.

Carl Icahn.blurb: The original corporate raider. Icahn built a $6B fortune by buying into companies, gutting their workforces, raiding their pension funds, and walking away with the proceeds.

Chamath Palihapitiya.blurb: Venture capitalist, SPAC king, and All-In podcast host who told the world he doesn't care about the Uyghur genocide. Co-owned a stake in the Golden State Warriors.

Charles Koch.blurb: The quiet architect of the modern anti-regulatory movement. Spent over $145 million funding climate denial, union busting, and anti-voter legislation while collecting hundreds of millions in government subsidies.

David Koch.blurb: Co-owner of Koch Industries and the largest individual funder of climate change denial in history — outspending ExxonMobil 3:1. Died in 2019 as the planet hit record temperatures.

Elon Musk.blurb: The world's richest person. Bought Twitter to silence critics, used DOGE to dismantle consumer protections that regulated his own companies, and called for abolishing Social Security.

Jack Ma.blurb: Alibaba founder who told workers that 12-hour, 6-day weeks were a 'blessing.' Disappeared from public life for three months after criticizing Chinese regulators.

Jamie Dimon.blurb: CEO of JPMorgan — the most profitable bank in US history on his watch. Has told workers to 'get over it,' described recessions as 'hurricanes,' and ordered everyone back to the office while Zooming in himself.

Jeff Bezos.blurb: Amazon founder who described work-life balance as 'debilitating,' called workers 'sickly gazelles,' and built a distribution empire on poverty wages, union busting, and surveillance of bathroom breaks.

Ken Griffin.blurb: Citadel hedge fund founder and Republican mega-donor who relocated his firm from Chicago to escape progressive governance. Spent record sums trying to buy elections while calling others 'entitled.'

Ken Langone.blurb: Home Depot co-founder who warned the Pope that criticizing wealth inequality would cost the Church its billionaire donors.

Kevin O'Leary.blurb: 'Mr. Wonderful.' Reality TV investor on Shark Tank who openly celebrates the brutal mechanics of capitalism and has called money an 'unfeeling' entity that eats the weak.

Larry Ellison.blurb: Oracle founder who told investors in 2024 that AI surveillance would keep 'citizens on their best behavior' through constant recording. Now involved in TikTok's US operations.

Lloyd Blankfein.blurb: Goldman Sachs CEO who received a $10B taxpayer bailout in 2008, paid out the largest bonuses in Goldman's history two weeks later, and told the Sunday Times he was 'doing God's work.'

Marc Andreessen.blurb: Co-founder of the most powerful VC firm in Silicon Valley. Wrote a 5,000-word 'Techno-Optimist Manifesto' listing scientists, regulators, and safety researchers as 'enemies.' Allegedly told a journalist he was glad OxyContin existed to keep rural Americans quiet.

Mark Zuckerberg.blurb: Meta CEO who eliminated fact-checkers two weeks before Trump's inauguration, after years of allowing Facebook to be weaponized for genocide and election interference. Now training in MMA.

Michael Bloomberg.blurb: Bloomberg LP founder and former NYC mayor whose own staff compiled a booklet of his workplace philosophy — the opening line was 'make the customer think he's getting laid when he's getting f*cked.'
Michael Bloomberg.tax_note: paid $0 in multiple years — ProPublica IRS files

Paul Singer.blurb: Elliott Management founder and 'vulture capitalist' who bought up the debt of struggling nations and companies, then sued them into bankruptcy to extract maximum profit.

Peter Thiel.blurb: PayPal and Palantir co-founder who said in 2009 that 'freedom and democracy are incompatible,' bankrolled the careers of JD Vance and Blake Masters, and secretly funded the lawsuit that destroyed Gawker. Has New Zealand citizenship and a compound there.
Peter Thiel.tax_note: grew Roth IRA $2K → $5B tax-free — ProPublica IRS files

Ray Dalio.blurb: Bridgewater Associates founder who built the world's largest hedge fund on a philosophy that employees are 'machines' to be optimized, and ran a workplace he called 'radical transparency' — critics called it a surveillance cult.

Richard Liu.blurb: JD.com founder who told employees that workers who don't put in extreme hours are 'slackers who are not my brothers.' Was acquitted of rape charges in the US after a $250M settlement.

Robert Mercer.blurb: Renaissance Technologies co-CEO and dark-money billionaire who bankrolled Breitbart, Cambridge Analytica, and Trump's 2016 campaign. Former colleagues say he believes people are only worth what they earn — welfare recipients have 'negative value.'

Sam Altman.blurb: OpenAI CEO who has said a catastrophic AI event 'might be the end of the world' — and also 'great for OpenAI.' Has reportedly purchased guns, gold, and land as insurance against the collapse of civilization.

Sam Bankman-Fried.blurb: FTX founder who built his brand as a philanthropy-driven 'effective altruist,' then admitted it was 'a front' after collapsing the exchange and wiping out $8 billion in customer funds. Sentenced to 25 years in prison.

Sam Walton.blurb: Walmart founder whose low-wage business model became the template for modern retail. Said openly: 'I pay low wages. I can take advantage of that.' The Walton family today has a combined net worth exceeding $250 billion.

Stephen Schwarzman.blurb: Blackstone co-founder who compared Obama's proposal to raise taxes on hedge funds to 'when Hitler invaded Poland.' Personal chef reportedly spent $3,000 per weekend on food including $40-per-claw stone crabs.

Steven Roth.blurb: Vornado Realty chairman who told a 2026 earnings call that 'tax the rich' is 'just as hateful as some disgusting racial slurs.' One of New York's largest commercial landlords.

Tom Perkins.blurb: Kleiner Perkins co-founder who compared progressive criticism of the wealthy to 'Nazi Germany's war on its 1 percent, namely its Jews.' The WSJ letter prompted his own VC firm to disavow him.

Travis Kalanick.blurb: Uber co-founder who built the gig economy's flagship company through regulatory evasion, driver exploitation, and a toxic internal culture. Forced out as CEO in 2017 after a cascade of scandals.

Vivek Ramaswamy.blurb: Pharma entrepreneur and DOGE co-chair who argued at Harvard that janitors shouldn't get raises, and told 116 million people in 2024 that American workers are too lazy and mediocre for the tech industry.

Prince Andrew (Duke of York).blurb: King Charles III's disgraced younger brother, stripped of titles in 2022. Had a years-long friendship with Jeffrey Epstein and stayed at his mansion after Epstein's sex crimes conviction, calling it 'convenient.' His 2019 BBC Newsnight interview defending himself became one of the most catastrophic PR failures in royal history.

Mohammed bin Salman.blurb: Saudi Crown Prince and de facto ruler who, per US intelligence, ordered the murder and dismemberment of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi in 2018. Has imprisoned thousands of dissidents, overseen a catastrophic war in Yemen, and spent hundreds of billions buying global legitimacy through sport and investment.

Leslie Wexner.blurb: L Brands (Victoria's Secret, Bath & Body Works) founder who gave Jeffrey Epstein near-total power of attorney over his finances and estate for years. Wexner later claimed he'd been 'defrauded' by Epstein — but the arrangement had made Epstein one of the most powerful men in New York.

Sergey Brin.blurb: Google co-founder named in Epstein files, with documents showing email correspondence with Ghislaine Maxwell. Multiple sources previously claimed he visited Epstein's island.

Gina Rinehart.blurb: Australia's richest person — an heiress who inherited mining tenements from her father and became worth $30B+ while publicly calling for workers to drink less and work more, and arguing Australian wages should compete with African workers earning $2/day.

Jensen Huang.blurb: Nvidia co-founder whose AI chip monopoly minted him $120B+ in personal wealth. Has publicly attacked AI safety researchers as spreading a 'doomer narrative' — while his chips power autonomous weapons systems and have repeatedly been found supplying Chinese military AI programs.

Bernard Arnault.blurb: LVMH chairman and Europe's richest person, who applied for Belgian citizenship when France proposed a wealth tax. His luxury empire — Dior, Vuitton, Loro Piana — has been repeatedly investigated for labor exploitation. Received $3.8B in personal compensation in 2025.

Warren Buffett.blurb: Berkshire Hathaway chairman who openly admitted 'my class is winning' the class war — then structured his own fortune to minimize taxes for decades while advocating in public for higher rates on the wealthy. Often cast as the 'good billionaire' for his folksy persona and pledge to give away wealth.
Warren Buffett.tax_note: $23.7M on $24.3B wealth gain — ProPublica IRS files

Larry Page.blurb: Google co-founder who publicly said he'd rather give his fortune to Elon Musk than to traditional charity. Has largely withdrawn from public life to a remote private island in Fiji, while Alphabet's ad dominance reshapes democracy and journalism.

Dan Loeb.blurb: Third Point hedge fund founder and Wall Street's self-described 'merchant of venom' — built a billion-dollar fortune by humiliating corporate executives in public SEC filings, calling them lazy socialites and members of the 'Lucky Sperm Club.'

Howard Lutnick.blurb: Cantor Fitzgerald CEO and current US Commerce Secretary who claimed he cut ties with Jeffrey Epstein in 2005, then was shown by Epstein files to have visited his private island in 2012, co-invested with him in 2013, and emailed him through 2018. Called Epstein 'the greatest blackmailer ever' before changing his story.

Alex Karp.blurb: Palantir CEO who built the world's most powerful private surveillance company, generating AI kill lists for the Israeli military, powering ICE's deportation machine, and consolidating Americans' private data for the Trump administration — all while earning $6.8B in personal compensation in 2024, the most of any US public company CEO.

Rupert Murdoch.blurb: News Corp and Fox Corporation founder who built a global media empire on disinformation, phone hacking, and propaganda. Under oath in the Dominion defamation case, he admitted Fox hosts 'endorsed' election lies he privately called 'bullsh*t,' and admitted he could have stopped it but chose not to. Fox settled for $787.5 million — the largest defamation settlement in US media history.

Reid Hoffman.blurb: LinkedIn co-founder and Greylock partner who confirmed that Silicon Valley billionaires use 'buying a house in New Zealand' as code for apocalypse insurance. Named in Epstein files for visiting the island and attending multiple Epstein dinners with Musk, Thiel, and Zuckerberg. Later called the association 'a mistake.'

Leon Black.blurb: Apollo Global Management founder who paid Jeffrey Epstein over $158 million between 2012 and 2017 — making him Epstein's single largest known source of funds — years after Epstein's conviction for soliciting a minor. Black called it 'estate planning.' DOJ files (2026) showed the relationship extended far beyond that.

Nelson Peltz.blurb: Trian Fund Management activist investor who asked 'why do I need an all-Black cast?' about Black Panther — which grossed $1.35 billion and was nominated for Best Picture. Peltz lost his proxy battle for Disney board seats. His $2B+ fortune is built on aggressive corporate raids that have gutted companies and their workforces.

Kim Kardashian.blurb: Reality TV billionaire who told struggling women 'get your f***ing ass up and work — nobody wants to work these days' in a Variety interview, while simultaneously being sued by seven household staff for unpaid wages and denied breaks. Her family's fame was launched by a leaked sex tape.

Oprah Winfrey.blurb: Media billionaire who told a Baltimore fundraising crowd that she didn't feel guilty about her wealth after visiting sub-Saharan Africa, saying she would go home to sleep on her $3,000 Pratesi sheets 'and feel good about it.'

Ellen DeGeneres.blurb: Comedian and talk show host who compared quarantining in her $27 million Montecito mansion to 'being in jail' as essential workers died on the job and prisoners contracted COVID-19 without protective equipment. Simultaneously, her show staff were going public with accounts of its toxic workplace.

Harold Hamm.blurb: Continental Resources founder and fracking billionaire who told the RNC that 'climate change isn't our biggest problem — it's Islamic terrorism,' organized a $1 billion quid-pro-quo pledge to Trump in exchange for gutting environmental protections, and reported more oil spills than any other North Dakota operator.

Lee Raymond.blurb: ExxonMobil CEO who publicly denied climate change for 12 years while his own scientists accurately tracked and predicted it since 1977. Funded $19M in climate denial organizations. Retired in 2005 with a $400M exit package — one of the largest in US corporate history.

Richard Sackler.blurb: Purdue Pharma president who called the 500,000+ Americans killed by OxyContin 'victimizers' and 'criminals.' His family extracted $11 billion from Purdue before declaring bankruptcy to dodge liability. He privately said he wanted to 'hammer on the abusers' while publicly insisting OxyContin was not addictive.

Martin Shkreli.blurb: Hedge fund manager and 'Pharma Bro' who raised a life-saving AIDS drug's price 5,455% overnight, then said at a Forbes conference that he 'probably should have raised it higher.' Convicted of securities fraud and sentenced to 7 years in prison.

Jonathan Gray.blurb: Blackstone's President and real estate chief, who explained the firm's housing strategy to Bloomberg in two words: 'Less supply is key.' Blackstone is one of the largest private landlords in America, concentrating housing ownership to drive up rents.

George Zoley.blurb: GEO Group founder and CEO who celebrated record profits from ICE detention on investor calls while children in his facilities were reportedly denied food and water. His company's entire business model depends on locking up more human beings.

Patrick Swindle.blurb: CoreCivic COO who called his company's detention facilities 'the most humane' on an investor earnings call — while his Dilley, Texas facility was simultaneously reported to be denying children medical care, clean water, and food, with a measles outbreak in progress.

Howard Schultz.blurb: Starbucks founder who returned as CEO specifically to crush the company's union drive, faced nearly 100 NLRB violations, and was found by a federal judge to have broken labor law by telling a pro-union barista to 'go work somewhere else.' Became $800M richer in a single year while baristas organized over low wages.

RealPage / Dana Jones.blurb: Algorithmic rent-setting company owned by private equity firm Thoma Bravo that the DOJ sued for running an illegal landlord pricing cartel. Its software manages over 24 million housing units globally and was found to coordinate rent increases across competing landlords using shared non-public data.

Angelo Mozilo.blurb: Countrywide Financial CEO who privately called his own mortgage products 'the most toxic product I've ever seen in my years in the business' and 'the poison of ours' in 2006 — while publicly assuring investors, homebuyers and regulators they were sound. Millions of families lost their homes. He settled SEC fraud charges for $67.5 million. No criminal charges.

William Campbell.blurb: Philip Morris CEO who swore to Congress in 1994 that nicotine is not addictive — while his company's own scientists had known since the 1960s that it was, and had been designing products to deliver it more efficiently. Philip Morris was later convicted under the RICO Act.

Seven Tobacco CEOs (1994).blurb: Seven tobacco company CEOs who swore to Congress in 1994 that nicotine is not addictive — while all of their companies had internal documents going back to 1963 confirming it was. All were investigated for perjury. The tobacco industry killed approximately 100 million people in the 20th century.

Addison Yeaman.blurb: Brown & Williamson general counsel who wrote in 1963 that the tobacco company was 'in the business of selling nicotine, an addictive drug' — 31 years before his industry's CEOs swore to Congress the opposite. The document was suppressed for decades.

Roger Goodell.blurb: NFL Commissioner who said in a 2022 deposition that there was 'still a great deal of uncertainty' about whether concussions cause brain damage — while the NFL had already paid $1.3 billion to former players with CTE, and BU's brain bank had found CTE in 96% of the NFL players' brains they had studied. Earns $60–70M/year.

Dan Snyder.blurb: Washington Commanders owner who presided over a 20-year culture of sexual harassment, hired private investigators to intimidate witnesses, gave misleading Congressional testimony, and improperly hid $11 million from NFL revenue sharing. Fined $60M — less than 1% of his $6B sale price.

Elizabeth Holmes.blurb: Theranos founder who told investors, doctors, and patients she had invented a revolutionary blood-testing device. She had not. Patients received false diagnoses. Engineers were silenced. Holmes was convicted of four counts of fraud in 2022 and sentenced to 11 years in prison.

Bernie Madoff.blurb: Ran the largest Ponzi scheme in history — $65 billion in fictitious returns, 37,000 victims. Said from prison that he wasn't 'some kind of ogre.' His son Mark killed himself on the second anniversary of his arrest. Madoff died in prison in 2021.

John Paulson.blurb: Paulson & Co. founder who allegedly helped Goldman Sachs design CDOs specifically structured to fail, then shorted them — earning $4 billion in a single year while pension funds lost the same amount. No charges were filed against Paulson. Goldman settled for $550 million.

Robert Kraft.blurb: New England Patriots owner charged with soliciting prostitution at a Florida spa linked to a human trafficking investigation. Spent $61,000 fighting the charges on constitutional grounds. All charges dropped when court ruled evidence inadmissible. Donated $20M to Harvard Medical School the following year.

David Geffen.blurb: DreamWorks co-founder and music mogul who posted a sunset photo from his $590M superyacht to Instagram on March 28, 2020, as New York City was becoming the COVID-19 epicenter. Caption: 'Isolated at sea. Thinking of all those who are suffering.' He deleted it within hours.

Bob Iger.blurb: Disney CEO who told CNBC he 'probably didn't raise prices enough' at Disneyland, called striking writers' expectations 'not realistic' from a $4,000/night resort conference, and earned $31.6M — 556 times the median Disney worker's pay — the same year.

George Soros.blurb: Hedge fund billionaire and Open Society Foundations funder who paid zero federal income taxes three years in a row, per ProPublica's IRS files. Has been the target of antisemitic conspiracy theories for decades, used by the right as shorthand for 'global liberal elite.'
George Soros.tax_note: paid $0 federal income tax 3 years in a row — ProPublica IRS files

Michael Milken.blurb: The 'Junk Bond King' who earned $550M in a single year while running an insider trading and securities fraud operation at Drexel Burnham Lambert. Pleaded guilty to six felonies, served 22 months of a 10-year sentence. Pardoned by Trump in 2020. Now worth $6 billion.

Wilbur Ross.blurb: Private equity billionaire who built his fortune buying bankrupt companies and stripping their workers' pensions — eliminating $12B in steel worker retirement obligations alone. Owned the Sago Mine when 12 miners died, having bought it with knowledge of 208 safety violations. Served as Trump's Commerce Secretary while secretly retaining financial conflicts of interest, including ties to Putin's inner circle.

Jerry Jones.blurb: Dallas Cowboys owner and general manager who told players to stand 'toe on the line' at the anthem or not play, has never hired a Black head coach in 33 years of ownership, and was photographed in 1957 among white students blocking Black students from entering a desegregated school. He called himself a 'curious kid' and did not apologize.

Steve Cohen.blurb: SAC Capital founder whose firm pleaded guilty to the largest insider trading settlement in US history ($1.8B) while simultaneously issuing a statement that it 'never encouraged or tolerated insider trading.' Eight employees convicted. Cohen never personally charged. Returned managing billions through Point72 in 2018 and bought the New York Mets for $2.4B in 2020.

Rex Tillerson.blurb: ExxonMobil CEO who publicly argued humanity should 'adapt' to climate change rather than prevent it — while his own company's scientists were accurately modelling catastrophic warming. Funded climate denial organizations. Trump's Secretary of State. First act: eliminating the climate envoy. Fired by tweet.

Stan Kroenke.blurb: Sports franchise owner who moved the Rams out of St. Louis after secretly negotiating an LA stadium deal — leaving fans with nothing. Led Arsenal into the 2021 Super League breakaway that would have destroyed European football's open competition model. Refused to apologize when it collapsed. A jury later awarded St. Louis $790M against the NFL.

John Tyson.blurb: Tyson Foods chairman who published a Washington Post op-ed about worker safety four days before 1,031 of his Waterloo, Iowa workers tested positive for COVID-19 — 58% of the plant. Five died. Managers had organized an internal betting pool on how many workers would get infected.

Sheldon Adelson.blurb: Las Vegas Sands founder who spent $600M+ on Republican politics and Trump's campaigns, demanded the US Embassy move to Jerusalem as a condition of his 2016 donation, and publicly proposed the US nuke Iran's desert as an opening negotiating tactic. Largest single US political donor of his era. His wife Miriam donated $106M to Trump's 2024 campaign.

Steve Wynn.blurb: Wynn Resorts founder who called decades of documented sexual misconduct allegations from 150 employees 'preposterous,' resigned after the Wall Street Journal investigation, and was permanently barred from the casino industry after paying $10M to Nevada regulators. Was simultaneously the RNC's finance chairman.
